_meta.authoringNotes: Burden, epidemiology, and elimination progress from WHO Global Programme to Eliminate Lymphatic Filariasis 2023-2024 reports and WHO Fact Sheet 2024. Clinical syndromes per Taylor et al. Lancet 2010 (PMID 20739055), Addiss DG. Filaria J 2007 review, and Ottesen EA. Filaria J 2006 (PMID 16545138). Doxycycline anti-Wolbachia regimen per Hoerauf et al. Lancet 2003 (PMID 12907018). Triple-therapy IDA cure data from King et al. NEJM 2018 (PMID 30257210). India-specific burden from Government of India National Vector Borne Disease Control Programme and ICMR-VCRC Puducherry. Imaging (filarial dance sign) per Amaral et al. AJTMH 1994.

h1Title: Filariasis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Lymphatic filariasis is a chronic mosquito-borne parasitic infection caused by thread-like nematodes (Wuchereria bancrofti, Brugia malayi, and Brugia timori) that lodge in human lymphatic vessels, where adult worm pairs live for 5-7 years and release millions of microfilariae into the bloodstream. WHO estimates that 859 million people across 50 countries remain at risk and roughly 51 million are infected, with India accounting for nearly 40% of the global burden. Most infected individuals carry the parasite without overt disease, but a substantial minority develop disfiguring lymphedema of the legs, breasts, or arms (elephantiasis), genital hydrocele, or chyluria after years of repeated infection. Treatment combines preventive chemotherapy for whole communities (diethylcarbamazine, ivermectin, and albendazole in various regimens) with morbidity management — meticulous skin hygiene, leg elevation, antibiotics for acute attacks, and surgery for hydrocele. Since the launch of the Global Programme to Eliminate Lymphatic Filariasis in 2000, prevalence has fallen by more than 75%.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 51 million people infected; 859 million in 50 countries at risk (WHO 2024); India 40% of global burden across 14 endemic states
keyStats.demographics: All ages affected; chronic manifestations (lymphedema, hydrocele) cluster in adults over 30 after years of repeated infection; men disproportionately affected by hydrocele
keyStats.avgAge: Microfilaremia detectable from late childhood; chronic disease typically appears age 30-50
keyStats.globalCases: Roughly 36 million people live with chronic disability — 25 million men with hydrocele and 15 million people with lymphedema/elephantiasis (WHO GPELF 2023)

definition: Filariasis (ICD-10: B74) is a group of parasitic infections caused by filarial nematode worms transmitted by blood-feeding arthropods. Lymphatic filariasis — the dominant clinical syndrome — is caused by three species: Wuchereria bancrofti (responsible for around 90% of cases globally, transmitted predominantly by Culex quinquefasciatus and Anopheles mosquitoes), Brugia malayi (Southeast Asia, parts of India, transmitted by Mansonia and Anopheles), and Brugia timori (Lesser Sunda islands of Indonesia). Adult worms 5-10 cm long inhabit lymphatic vessels and lymph nodes, where mating pairs produce microfilariae 200-300 micrometers long that circulate in peripheral blood — generally with strict nocturnal periodicity for W. bancrofti and B. malayi. Cumulative damage to the lymphatic endothelium from worm presence, immune response, and bacterial superinfection drives progressive lymphedema, hydrocele, and tropical pulmonary eosinophilia. Beyond lymphatic filariasis, two related diseases caused by tissue-dwelling filariae are sometimes grouped under the term: onchocerciasis (river blindness, Onchocerca volvulus) and loiasis (the eye worm, Loa loa). These have distinct presentations and are treated as separate entities. Filariasis is managed by tropical medicine, infectious disease, lymphology, and urology services, and at population scale by national vector-borne disease programmes coordinated through the WHO Global Programme to Eliminate Lymphatic Filariasis.

typesClassification.description: Microfilariae circulate in blood without overt symptoms; up to 50% of infected individuals fall in this group. Subclinical lymphatic dilatation is detectable on ultrasound and renal involvement on urinalysis.

Acute adenolymphangitis (ADL) — episodes of fever, painful lymphadenitis, and retrograde lymphangitis lasting 3-7 days, often triggered by bacterial superinfection of compromised lymphatics. Filarial fever and acute funiculo-epididymo-orchitis also occur.

Progressive non-pitting swelling of one or both lower limbs (sometimes upper limbs or breasts) graded 1-4 in severity, culminating in hyperkeratotic, fibrotic, fissured skin (true elephantiasis). Develops after years of repeated lymphatic damage and recurrent ADL.

typesClassification.type: Hydrocele and genital lymphatic disease (W. bancrofti)
typesClassification.description: Adult worms in the spermatic lymphatics dilate and obstruct testicular lymph drainage, producing painless scrotal swelling — the most common chronic manifestation of W. bancrofti infection, affecting roughly 25 million men globally.

Rupture of dilated retroperitoneal lymphatics into the urinary tract releases lymphatic fluid (chyle), producing milky urine, weight loss, hypoalbuminemia, and recurrent urinary tract infection. More common with W. bancrofti.

Hyperreactive immune response to microfilariae trapped in pulmonary capillaries. Presents with nocturnal cough, wheeze, breathlessness, eosinophilia above 3,000/µL, and elevated IgE; mistaken for asthma or chronic bronchitis until filarial serology is checked.

primarySymptoms: Painless rubbery enlargement of inguinal, axillary, or epitrochlear lymph nodes from adult worm nesting in draining lymphatic vessels.

Episodes of high fever (39-40 °C), rigors, and exquisitely tender red streaks tracking from a limb into a swollen lymph node — acute dermatolymphangioadenitis (ADL), typically lasting 3-7 days and recurring every few weeks to months.
Painless, progressive scrotal swelling that transilluminates — hydrocele caused by lymphatic obstruction at the spermatic cord; bilateral in roughly one-third of severely affected men.

Non-pitting swelling of one or both legs that progresses from soft, reversible edema (Stage 1) to hyperkeratotic, fibrotic, fissured skin with deep skinfolds and mossy lesions (Stage 4 elephantiasis).
Milky-white urine, particularly after fatty meals, with or without microscopic hematuria — chyluria from rupture of dilated lymphatics into the urinary tract.
Nocturnal paroxysmal cough, wheeze, and breathlessness in a young adult from an endemic region — tropical pulmonary eosinophilia, often misdiagnosed as asthma.
Acute funiculitis, epididymitis, or orchitis with painful scrotal swelling separate from hydrocele.
Recurrent cellulitis, lymphangitis, or fungal infection in a chronically swollen limb caused by bacterial entry through compromised skin barriers.
Breast enlargement with thickened, peau-d'orange skin in women with axillary lymphatic involvement (filarial mastitis or breast lymphedema).
Visible motile adult worm pairs on scrotal ultrasound — the filarial dance sign — pathognomonic for adult Wuchereria worms.
earlyWarningSigns: Recurrent low-grade fever with painful inguinal or axillary lymphadenopathy in a child or young adult from an endemic district
Episodic single-leg or scrotal swelling that fully resolves between attacks early in disease
Peripheral eosinophilia above 500/µL in an asymptomatic resident of an endemic area
Mild non-pitting ankle swelling on the dominant leg that worsens after long periods of standing
Recurrent cellulitis of the lower leg in someone with no diabetes or venous disease
emergencySigns: High fever, rigors, and rapidly spreading erythema with systemic toxicity — bacterial sepsis from compromised lymphatics; needs urgent intravenous antibiotics
Sudden severe scrotal pain with swelling — exclude testicular torsion or strangulated inguinal hernia before assuming hydrocele or filarial epididymitis
Severe breathlessness, wheeze, and hypoxia in suspected tropical pulmonary eosinophilia — exclude asthma exacerbation or anaphylaxis
Massive sudden lymphedema or skin necrosis after deworming — Mazzotti-like or DEC-reaction in heavily microfilaremic individuals
Acute neurological deterioration during DEC or ivermectin treatment in someone who may also have Loa loa — risk of encephalopathy

causes.description: Responsible for around 90% of human lymphatic filariasis globally. Adult worms inhabit lymphatic vessels and lymph nodes draining the legs, genitals, and breasts. Microfilariae circulate in peripheral blood with strict nocturnal periodicity in most endemic regions, matching the biting time of the principal vector Culex quinquefasciatus.

causes.cause: Infection with Brugia malayi or Brugia timori
causes.description: Cause most cases in Southeast Asia and pockets of India and Indonesia. Brugia species tend to produce lymphedema below the knee and avoid genital involvement. Mansonia and Anopheles mosquitoes are the main vectors.

causes.cause: Mosquito vector bite delivering infective L3 larvae
causes.description: When an infected mosquito feeds, third-stage larvae enter through the bite wound, migrate to regional lymphatics over weeks, and mature into adult worms 6-18 months later. Repeated exposure across years builds up adult worm burden.

causes.cause: Cumulative lymphatic damage from years of infection
causes.description: Adult worm presence, host immune response, and secondary bacterial entry through damaged skin progressively destroy lymphatic endothelium and valves, producing irreversible dilatation, fibrosis, and chronic lymphedema.

causes.cause: Wolbachia endosymbiont bacteria within filarial worms
causes.description: All Wuchereria and Brugia adult worms carry obligate intracellular Wolbachia bacteria essential for worm fertility and survival. Wolbachia drive much of the chronic inflammatory pathology and are the target of doxycycline therapy.

Cuts, fungal infections, and inter-digital cracks allow streptococci and staphylococci to enter compromised lymphatics, triggering ADL attacks that further damage lymphatic architecture and accelerate elephantiasis.

riskFactors.description: Lymphatic filariasis is endemic across 50 countries in sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, Southeast Asia, the Pacific, and parts of the Americas. India alone accounts for roughly 40% of the global burden, with 14 endemic states.

Farmers, sanitary workers, fishermen, and night-shift outdoor workers in endemic areas accumulate more infective bites. Culex mosquitoes (principal vectors of W. bancrofti) breed in poorly drained water, sewage, and pit latrines.

Open drains, stagnant water, lack of window screens, and poor sanitation favor mosquito breeding. WHO surveillance shows higher microfilaremia in low-income urban slums than in well-drained rural villages.
Hydrocele is almost exclusively a male manifestation; in heavily endemic communities, hydrocele prevalence in men over 40 can exceed 20%.
Chronic disease requires sustained exposure: single trips for travelers carry low risk of overt filariasis, while continuous residence over decades drives heavy worm burdens.

riskFactors.factor: Lack of access to mass drug administration
riskFactors.description: Communities missed by annual MDA (migrant settlements, conflict zones, remote islands) retain reservoirs of microfilaremia and ongoing transmission.

riskFactors.factor: Co-infection with Onchocerca volvulus or Loa loa
riskFactors.description: Affects safety of treatment regimens — DEC is contraindicated in onchocerciasis and dangerous in heavy Loa loa infection because of risk of Mazzotti reaction and encephalopathy.

affectedDemographics: India: roughly 600 million at risk across 256 endemic districts; 14 states involved including Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, Andhra Pradesh, Odisha, Tamil Nadu, and the northeast.
Sub-Saharan Africa: high burden in Nigeria, Democratic Republic of Congo, Ethiopia, Tanzania, and Cameroon.
Southeast Asia: persistent transmission in Indonesia, Myanmar, Timor-Leste, and parts of the Philippines.
Pacific: smaller populations on Pacific islands (Samoa, Fiji, Tonga, Papua New Guinea) — Samoa progressed to verification of elimination as a public-health problem in 2017.
Americas: Haiti, Dominican Republic, Brazil, and Guyana — Haiti remains the largest endemic country in the region.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines clinical pattern recognition with parasitological or antigen tests. In active microfilaremia, a thick blood film stained with Giemsa and collected during the species-specific peak periodicity (typically between 10 p.m. and 2 a.m. for W. bancrofti) reveals microfilariae and identifies the species by sheath, tail, and nuclear features. Concentration techniques (Knott's method, membrane filtration) improve sensitivity in light infections. The rapid Filariasis Test Strip and circulating filarial antigen (CFA) assay (ICT card and Og4C3 ELISA) detect Wuchereria bancrofti antigen at any time of day without nocturnal sampling and are now standard for both diagnosis and population-level mapping; they do not, however, detect Brugia species. Anti-filarial antibody serology (IgG4 to BmR1 or BmA antigens) is useful for Brugia and for low-burden infections in travelers. Scrotal ultrasound demonstrates the pathognomonic filarial dance sign — characteristic chaotic motion of adult worms inside dilated lymphatic vessels — and is useful for confirming live worms before treatment. PCR can detect parasite DNA in blood, urine, and tissue when microscopy and antigen tests are negative. In suspected tropical pulmonary eosinophilia, the diagnostic triad is residence in or travel from an endemic area, peripheral eosinophilia above 3,000/µL with elevated IgE, and dramatic clinical response to DEC.

diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood is drawn between 10 p.m. and 2 a.m. for nocturnally periodic species (most W. bancrofti and B. malayi). Two thick films are stained and examined under microscopy. Result usually available the same day. Repeat sampling may be required for light infections.

diagnosticTests.test: Circulating filarial antigen test (ICT card / Og4C3 ELISA)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects adult W. bancrofti antigen — daytime sample, more sensitive than microscopy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fingerprick or venous blood sample. ICT cassette reads in 10-15 minutes; Og4C3 ELISA returns in 1-3 days. Does not detect Brugia species.

diagnosticTests.test: Anti-filarial IgG4 serology (BmR1 or BmA antigens)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects antibody response to Brugia or Wuchereria; especially useful for low-burden infection and Brugia species
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw. Result within 3-7 days. Positive antibody can persist after cure and does not separate active from past infection.

diagnosticTests.test: Scrotal or lymphatic ultrasound (filarial dance sign)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Visualizes live adult worms in dilated lymphatic vessels and confirms active W. bancrofti infection
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard ultrasound scan of the scrotum or affected lymphatic territory, 15-20 minutes. Sonographer looks for the characteristic chaotic flicker of motile worms.

diagnosticTests.test: Pulmonary function testing and chest X-ray with eosinophil count
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Spirometry, full blood count, total IgE, and chest imaging. Pattern: bilateral diffuse mottling, restrictive or mixed defect, eosinophilia >3,000/µL, IgE >1,000 IU/mL.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirmatory or low-burden diagnosis when microscopy and antigen testing are negative
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood, urine (chyluria), or tissue sample sent to a reference laboratory. Result within 1-2 weeks.

treatmentOverview: Treatment runs at two levels: individual patient care for the active worm infection and for chronic morbidity, and population-level preventive chemotherapy to interrupt transmission. For individuals, oral diethylcarbamazine (DEC) 6 mg/kg over 12 days clears microfilariae and partially kills adult worms; ivermectin (200 µg/kg) and albendazole (400 mg) are alternatives or partners. Triple-therapy IDA (ivermectin + DEC + albendazole) given as a single annual dose clears microfilaremia for at least two years in over 95% of patients and is the WHO-preferred MDA regimen in areas free of onchocerciasis and Loa loa. Doxycycline 200 mg/day for 4-6 weeks targets the obligate Wolbachia endosymbiont, sterilizes adult worms within 3-6 months, and over time kills them — uniquely macrofilaricidal and especially useful in early lymphedema. Mass drug administration delivered annually for 5-7 years has cut prevalence by more than 75% globally since 2000 and verified elimination as a public-health problem in 19 countries. Morbidity management and disability prevention (MMDP) is critical for those with established disease: meticulous skin hygiene, hot-water washing, antifungal and antibacterial treatment of inter-digital lesions, leg elevation, compression bandaging, and prompt antibiotics for ADL attacks reduce the frequency of acute episodes by more than 80%. Hydrocele responds to scrotal surgery (hydrocelectomy), which restores function in over 90% of operated men. For tropical pulmonary eosinophilia, DEC 6 mg/kg/day for 14-21 days resolves symptoms in over 90% of cases, with improvement starting within days.

medicalTreatments.name: Diethylcarbamazine (DEC, 6 mg/kg orally over 12 days or single dose with albendazole)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line microfilaricide; partially macrofilaricidal. Side-effects (fever, headache, myalgia) are immune reactions to dying microfilariae. Contraindicated in heavy onchocerciasis (risk of Mazzotti reaction) and dangerous in Loa loa hyperinfection.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Microfilariae cleared in over 95% of patients within 1-2 weeks; long-term clearance higher with annual repeat dosing

medicalTreatments.name: Ivermectin 200 µg/kg single dose
medicalTreatments.description: Rapid microfilaricide used in MDA, especially in Africa where onchocerciasis co-circulates. Does not kill adult worms. Now combined with DEC and albendazole in IDA regimens in suitable countries.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Microfilariae cleared within days; recrudescence in 6-12 months without repeat dosing

medicalTreatments.name: Albendazole 400 mg single dose
medicalTreatments.description: Partner drug in DEC+albendazole and IDA regimens. Modestly macrofilaricidal at extended dosing. Also clears soil-transmitted helminths, an added benefit in MDA.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adds modestly to microfilarial clearance; the main rationale is partnership effect and broader anthelmintic activity

medicalTreatments.name: Triple therapy IDA (ivermectin + DEC + albendazole) single dose
medicalTreatments.description: WHO-preferred MDA regimen in areas free of onchocerciasis and Loa loa, given annually. Single round can achieve sustained microfilarial clearance for 24+ months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cleared microfilaremia in 96% at 12 months vs 32% with DEC+albendazole in King NEJM 2018 (PMID 30257210); now scaled in India and Pacific

medicalTreatments.name: Doxycycline 200 mg/day for 4-6 weeks (anti-Wolbachia)
medicalTreatments.description: Targets obligate Wolbachia endosymbiont. Sterilizes adult worms quickly and kills them within 12-24 months. Uniquely macrofilaricidal and may reverse early lymphedema. Avoid in pregnancy and children under 8.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adult worm death in 70-80% at 18 months; improvement in lymphedema severity in 30-50% with early disease

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotics for acute dermatolymphangioadenitis (penicillin, amoxicillin-clavulanate)
medicalTreatments.description: Treat bacterial superinfection that drives ADL episodes. Early oral or intravenous antibiotics shorten attacks and limit lymphatic damage.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolution of acute attack within 3-5 days; long-term penicillin prophylaxis can reduce ADL frequency by 50-80% in selected patients

surgicalOptions.name: Hydrocelectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Excision or window-eversion of the tunica vaginalis through a scrotal incision; day-case procedure in most settings. Restores normal scrotal contour and function.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic and cosmetic resolution in over 90% of patients; recurrence rate under 5% in modern technique

surgicalOptions.name: Lymphatic-venous anastomosis or lymphaticovenular bypass (microsurgery)
surgicalOptions.description: Microsurgical reconnection of patent lymphatics to local veins to relieve obstruction; offered in specialist lymphology centres for early- to mid-stage lymphedema.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Volume reduction of 30-60% in selected patients; durable improvement in roughly 60% at 3 years in published series

surgicalOptions.name: Debulking surgery (Charles, Homans, or staged excisional procedures)
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for very advanced elephantiasis with severe disability. Removes excess subcutaneous tissue and skin to restore mobility; functional rather than cosmetic.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Mobility and activities-of-daily-living improvement in roughly 70% of carefully selected patients; significant wound-healing morbidity

alternativeTreatments.name: Yoga and structured exercise programmes
alternativeTreatments.description: Programmes from the ICMR Vector Control Research Centre in Puducherry demonstrate reduction in limb volume and acute attack frequency with combined hygiene plus daily yoga and limb-elevation routines.

alternativeTreatments.name: Compression bandaging and graduated stockings (Class II-III, 23-32 mmHg)
alternativeTreatments.description: Multilayer compression bandaging followed by long-term graduated stockings is the cornerstone of conservative lymphedema therapy and is endorsed by the International Society of Lymphology.

whySeeSpecialist: Tropical medicine and infectious disease physicians confirm species, choose the safest treatment combination in regions where onchocerciasis or Loa loa coexist, and arrange follow-up parasitology. Lymphology, vascular surgery, urology, and physiotherapy services are essential for established hydrocele, advanced lymphedema, and disability rehabilitation.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a clinician based in or trained at a centre involved in WHO-endorsed elimination programmes — for example, ICMR-VCRC in India, Liverpool School of Tropical Medicine, or US CDC parasitic disease branch. For chronic disease, a multidisciplinary clinic with lymphology, urology, dermatology, and physiotherapy outperforms single-specialty care.
hospitalCriteria: Access to nocturnal blood smear parasitology and filarial antigen testing
Surgical capacity for hydrocelectomy and lymphatic surgery
Lymphedema therapist or lymphology service for compression bandaging
Infectious disease specialist familiar with DEC, ivermectin, albendazole, and doxycycline regimens
Linkage to local mass drug administration programme for community-level prevention
keyFacilities: ICMR Vector Control Research Centre (Puducherry, India)
National Vector-Borne Disease Control Programme units
Tertiary lymphology and vascular surgery services
preventionStrategies: Sleep under a long-lasting insecticidal net (LLIN) and use repellents in endemic regions, especially during peak Culex biting hours.
Treat household and neighborhood mosquito breeding sites (covered drains, stagnant water in containers, septic tanks).
Participate annually in mass drug administration when offered, even when asymptomatic — single-dose IDA or DEC + albendazole protects against transmission for 12+ months.
Cover exposed skin and use insect repellent during evening outdoor activity (DEET 30%, picaridin 20%, IR3535).
Screen and treat pregnant women in endemic regions for chronic anemia related to filarial infection.
Support sanitation upgrades and vector control at the community level — the strongest sustainable preventive measure.
lifestyleModifications: Wash affected limbs daily with soap, clean water, and antifungal cream as advised — the most evidence-based intervention to prevent ADL attacks.
Treat inter-digital fissures, fungal infection, and cuts immediately to prevent bacterial entry.
Elevate the affected limb above heart level for 20-30 minutes, two to three times daily, especially after standing.
Wear well-fitted graduated compression stockings (Class II-III) during waking hours; remove at night.
Maintain healthy weight — obesity worsens lymphedema and acute attack frequency.
Walk and exercise daily — gentle activity promotes lymphatic flow; avoid prolonged standing or heat exposure.

recommended: Balanced low-fat, high-protein diet to limit chyluria episodes when present
Iron- and folate-rich foods (lean meat, leafy greens, lentils) to combat chronic anemia
Adequate hydration (2-3 L/day) to support kidney function and reduce chyluria viscosity
Medium-chain triglyceride (MCT)-based fats in confirmed chyluria as they bypass the lymphatic system
avoid: Long-chain dietary fats during active chyluria — they exacerbate milky urine
Excess salt that worsens lower-limb edema
Alcohol that aggravates edema and interacts with DEC and doxycycline
Heavy fatty meals just before treatment, as nausea is more common

exerciseGuidelines: Daily walking, swimming in clean water, yoga, and gentle resistance training all improve lymphatic flow and reduce limb volume. Avoid heavy weight-bearing exercise during acute ADL attacks. Limb-specific exercises taught by a lymphedema therapist (toe-flexion, ankle-pumping, calf-pumping) used while wearing compression are particularly effective.
dailyManagement: Perform morning and evening limb hygiene (wash, dry between toes, apply antifungal or emollient).
Inspect skin daily for fissures, cuts, or fungal infection; treat early.
Wear correctly fitted compression stockings during the day.
Elevate the limb for 20-30 minutes twice daily and at night with a pillow.
Track ADL attacks, fever episodes, and antibiotic courses in a diary.
Attend annual MDA rounds in the community even when asymptomatic.
prognosis: Outlook depends mainly on how early the infection is detected and treated. With one or two annual rounds of preventive chemotherapy (DEC + albendazole or IDA), more than 95% of microfilaremic individuals clear circulating microfilariae and no longer transmit infection. Adult worms can take 2-4 years to die without doxycycline; with doxycycline 4-6 weeks, sterility and death of adult worms occur within 12-18 months. Early lymphedema (Stage 1-2) often regresses with combined antifilarial treatment and rigorous limb hygiene; Stage 3-4 elephantiasis is largely irreversible, but acute attacks fall by 80% and quality of life improves substantially with structured morbidity-management programmes. Hydrocele responds dramatically to surgery, with restoration of function in over 90%. Tropical pulmonary eosinophilia has an excellent prognosis if treated early — over 90% recover full pulmonary function with DEC — but delayed treatment leaves a fixed restrictive pattern in roughly 20%. Population-level elimination is feasible: 19 countries have been verified by WHO as having eliminated lymphatic filariasis as a public-health problem (2024 data), and India is on track for verification in several states.
recoveryTimeline: Microfilariae cleared within 1-2 weeks of DEC or IDA. ADL attacks decrease in frequency from monthly to once or twice yearly after 3-6 months of consistent skin hygiene. Early-stage lymphedema improves within 6-12 months. Adult worms killed by doxycycline die over 12-24 months. Tropical pulmonary eosinophilia resolves within 2-3 weeks of DEC. Hydrocelectomy recovery: return to work in 1-2 weeks, full healing in 6 weeks.
complications: Irreversible elephantiasis with severe disability, isolation, and depression — prevent with early hygiene programmes and MDA.
Recurrent bacterial sepsis from compromised lymphatics — track ADL frequency and pursue penicillin prophylaxis where attacks recur monthly or more.
Renal failure from chronic chyluria and immune-complex glomerulonephritis — surveillance urinalysis and renal function in symptomatic patients.
Hydrocele rupture or infection — refer for elective hydrocelectomy when size affects daily function.
Tropical pulmonary eosinophilia progressing to interstitial fibrosis if untreated for years.
Mental-health and economic morbidity from disfiguring disease — disability rates in established elephantiasis communities approach those of leprosy.

supportResources.name: WHO Global Programme to Eliminate Lymphatic Filariasis
supportResources.description: Global burden, elimination targets, and country-level progress reports.

supportResources.name: Government of India — National Centre for Vector Borne Diseases Control (NCVBDC)
supportResources.description: India-specific guidelines, district-level mapping, and MDA schedule.

supportResources.name: ICMR Vector Control Research Centre (VCRC) Puducherry
supportResources.description: Indian research and clinical hub for filariasis, including morbidity management training.

supportResources.name: Coalition for Operational Research on Neglected Tropical Diseases (COR-NTD)
supportResources.description: Research collaboration that publishes operational data and toolkits for filariasis programmes.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lymphedema
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Non-filarial lymphedema (post-mastectomy, congenital, post-surgical, obesity-related) lacks microfilariae, negative filarial antigen, and usually no history of endemic exposure. Filarial lymphedema typically begins distally and is associated with recurrent fever and ADL attacks; non-filarial forms are more often non-febrile.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hydrocele
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Non-filarial hydrocele (idiopathic, post-trauma, post-infection) lacks adult worm visualization on ultrasound and shows no filarial antigen. Filarial hydrocele typically occurs in a man from an endemic region and may show the filarial dance sign on scrotal ultrasound.

confusedWithConditions.name: Schistosomiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both are tropical helminth infections with chronic genital morbidity. Schistosomiasis causes hematuria and bladder fibrosis (S. haematobium) or hepatosplenomegaly (S. mansoni); filariasis causes lymphedema, hydrocele, and chyluria. Diagnosis is by stool/urine egg microscopy or schistosome serology, with treatment by praziquantel rather than DEC.

confusedWithConditions.name: Onchocerciasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Onchocerca volvulus produces subcutaneous nodules, intensely itchy onchodermatitis, and visual loss (river blindness). Microfilariae are found in skin snips rather than blood. Treatment is ivermectin; DEC is contraindicated because of severe Mazzotti reaction.

relatedConditions.name: Schistosomiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-endemic tropical helminth disease that shares MDA platforms and integrated NTD programmes.

relatedConditions.name: Malaria
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares mosquito-borne transmission (Anopheles), endemic geography, and febrile presentation; commonly screened together.

relatedConditions.name: Leishmaniasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another vector-borne NTD with overlapping endemic countries; managed by similar tropical medicine services.

relatedConditions.name: Strongyloidiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Helminth co-infection that also features eosinophilia and is screened in returning travelers.

relatedConditions.name: Lymphedema
relatedConditions.relevance: Underlying mechanism (lymphatic obstruction) and conservative management overlap with filarial lymphedema.

faqs.question: What is filariasis in simple terms?
faqs.answer: Filariasis is a parasitic disease caused by thread-like worms transmitted by mosquitoes. The most common form, lymphatic filariasis, damages the body's drainage system and can cause severe swelling of the legs, breasts, or genitals over many years. Treatment combines deworming drugs with strict skin hygiene and supportive care.

faqs.question: How is filariasis spread?
faqs.answer: Filariasis spreads through bites of infected Culex, Anopheles, Mansonia, or Aedes mosquitoes. The mosquito injects infective larvae that migrate to lymphatic vessels, where they mature into adult worms over 6-18 months. It is not contagious between people. Repeated exposure over years is needed to develop chronic disease.

faqs.question: What does filariasis look like in early stages?
faqs.answer: Most people are initially asymptomatic. Early features include episodes of fever with painful lymph node swelling (ADL), mild non-pitting ankle edema, recurrent cellulitis on one leg, or unexplained eosinophilia on routine blood tests. Many cases are detected only by blood tests during community surveys.

faqs.question: What is the difference between filariasis and elephantiasis?
faqs.answer: Filariasis is the underlying parasitic infection. Elephantiasis is the late, irreversible consequence — gross thickening and disfigurement of an affected limb caused by years of lymphatic damage, recurrent bacterial infection, and fibrosis. Not all filariasis progresses to elephantiasis, especially with early treatment and good hygiene.

faqs.question: Is filariasis curable?
faqs.answer: Active infection is curable with deworming drugs that kill microfilariae in over 95% of patients and adult worms over 12-24 months when doxycycline is added. Established elephantiasis is not fully reversible, but acute attacks can be reduced by more than 80% with hygiene-based morbidity management.

faqs.question: What drugs treat filariasis?
faqs.answer: First-line drugs are diethylcarbamazine (DEC), ivermectin, and albendazole, often combined as a single-dose triple therapy (IDA) in WHO-endorsed mass treatment. Doxycycline 200 mg/day for 4-6 weeks targets the Wolbachia bacteria inside the worm and is uniquely able to kill adult parasites.

faqs.question: Why is filariasis treatment given at night?
faqs.answer: The microfilariae of Wuchereria bancrofti and Brugia malayi rise into peripheral blood mainly between 10 p.m. and 2 a.m. to match the biting habits of their mosquito vectors. Blood smears taken at this time have the highest yield. Antigen tests and PCR have removed this limitation.

faqs.question: Can children get filariasis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Microfilaremia is regularly detected in children as young as 5 years in endemic regions, and chronic lymphedema can begin in adolescence with heavy exposure. Mass drug administration includes school-age children at age-appropriate doses. Doxycycline is avoided under age 8.

faqs.question: What is mass drug administration for filariasis?
faqs.answer: Mass drug administration (MDA) is annual treatment of entire endemic populations regardless of symptoms. Standard regimens are DEC + albendazole, or ivermectin + albendazole where onchocerciasis is co-endemic, or triple therapy IDA. Five to seven yearly rounds typically interrupt transmission. Over 100 million people have received MDA in India alone.

faqs.question: How do I prevent filariasis?
faqs.answer: Sleep under an insecticide-treated bed net, use mosquito repellents at dusk and dawn, eliminate stagnant water around the home, and participate in annual community deworming campaigns. Travelers to endemic regions for short trips face very low individual risk; long-term residents and workers should use vector-control measures.

faqs.question: Is filariasis a problem in India?
faqs.answer: Yes. India accounts for roughly 40% of the global lymphatic filariasis burden, with 14 endemic states and around 600 million people at risk. The Government of India National Vector-Borne Disease Control Programme runs annual MDA rounds with IDA in target districts and aims for elimination as a public-health problem by 2027.

faqs.question: Why does filariasis cause hydrocele?
faqs.answer: Adult Wuchereria bancrofti worms live in the lymphatic vessels around the testis and spermatic cord. Over years they dilate and obstruct these vessels, causing lymph and fluid to accumulate within the tunica vaginalis around the testis. The resulting hydrocele is the most common chronic manifestation of W. bancrofti infection.

faqs.question: What is tropical pulmonary eosinophilia?
faqs.answer: Tropical pulmonary eosinophilia (TPE) is a hyperreactive immune response to microfilariae trapped in pulmonary capillaries. It causes nocturnal cough, wheeze, and breathlessness in young adults from endemic regions, with eosinophilia over 3,000/µL and IgE over 1,000 IU/mL. It responds dramatically to DEC.

faqs.question: Can travelers catch filariasis?
faqs.answer: Brief travel rarely produces overt disease because chronic filariasis requires sustained, repeated exposure. Aid workers, missionaries, military personnel, and long-stay expatriates in endemic regions are at higher risk. Travelers with persistent eosinophilia, fevers, or limb swelling after an endemic stay should be screened with filarial antigen and serology.

faqs.question: What is the filarial dance sign?
faqs.answer: The filarial dance sign is the chaotic, flickering motion of adult Wuchereria worms inside dilated lymphatic vessels seen on scrotal or breast ultrasound. It is pathognomonic for active W. bancrofti infection and is used to confirm live adult worms before macrofilaricidal treatment.

faqs.question: Why is daily skin care so important in filariasis?
faqs.answer: Repeated bacterial infection of cracked or fungally infected skin is the main driver of acute attacks and progression to elephantiasis. Twice-daily washing with soap and water, antifungal care between toes, and prompt treatment of any cut or blister reduces ADL frequency by more than 80% in WHO morbidity-management programmes.

faqs.question: Does filariasis affect pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Active microfilaremia is associated with chronic anemia and lower birth weight but does not cross the placenta to infect the fetus directly. DEC and albendazole are avoided in pregnancy where possible; deferral to postpartum is preferred. Doxycycline is contraindicated.

faqs.question: Is filariasis the same as river blindness?
faqs.answer: No. River blindness (onchocerciasis) is caused by Onchocerca volvulus and transmitted by black flies, not mosquitoes. It produces skin nodules, severe itch, and blindness, with microfilariae in skin rather than blood. DEC is contraindicated in onchocerciasis; ivermectin is the standard treatment.

faqs.question: Can filariasis come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Microfilariae can return without further treatment if adult worms remain alive in the lymphatics and if re-exposure continues. Annual MDA prevents this. Doxycycline targets adult worms and reduces relapse risk substantially. Travelers without further endemic exposure rarely relapse after cure.

faqs.question: Does filariasis have a vaccine?
faqs.answer: No licensed vaccine exists for human lymphatic filariasis. Several candidate vaccines targeting adult worm and infective-larval antigens are in preclinical or early clinical development, but none is available outside of trials. Control depends on vector management, mass drug administration, and morbidity management.

faqs.question: What follow-up is needed after filariasis treatment?
faqs.answer: Repeat blood antigen or microfilarial testing at 6 and 12 months confirms parasitological cure; clinical assessment of limb volume and ADL frequency tracks morbidity. Annual MDA rounds continue at the community level. Travelers without re-exposure usually need no further follow-up after a documented cure.

metaDescription: Filariasis (lymphatic filariasis, elephantiasis): mosquito-borne parasite causing leg and genital swelling. Treatment, prevention, MDA — medically reviewed.
